claim: FAO's first Global Assessment of Soil Carbon in Grasslands (2023) finds grasslands hold about 20 percent of the world's soil organic carbon stocks and reports a negative carbon balance — declining stocks indicative of degradation pressure — in several regions including Africa south of the Equator, Central and South America, and East Asia.

source.title: Global assessment of soil carbon in grasslands

method: Re-sourced after the originally cited 'Status of the World's Grasslands' link rotted (HTTP 404) and could not be confirmed to exist under that title; replaced with FAO's verifiable 2023 grassland soil-carbon assessment and rewrote the claim to match what that report actually supports.
limitations: This report measures soil organic carbon stocks and balance, not a direct percent-degraded statistic; the prior '20-30 percent degraded' figure was not supported by a verifiable FAO source and has been removed.
Regional carbon-balance findings do not single out Central Asia; a Central-Asia-specific degradation-extent figure still needs a dedicated source.
Soil-carbon balance is one proxy for degradation; it does not capture vegetation cover loss, biodiversity, or pastoral livelihood impacts.
Estimates are modeled from global datasets with country-level uncertainty.

claim: MODIS NDVI at 250m resolution provides continuous vegetation-productivity monitoring from 2000 to present, enabling trend analysis of grassland condition. The Rangeland Analysis Platform and similar products map fractional vegetation cover change at 30m resolution from Landsat data.
method: Reviewed MODIS NDVI product documentation for spatial resolution, temporal coverage, compositing method, and known limitations in arid and semi-arid rangelands. Cross-referenced with Rangeland Analysis Platform methodology.
limitations: NDVI at 250m resolution may not capture paddock-scale heterogeneity relevant to herder decision-making — localized degradation around wells and settlements may be averaged with productive areas.

NDVI responds to both precipitation variability and persistent degradation — disambiguating climate-driven productivity fluctuations from irreversible degradation requires multi-decade time series and statistical trend methods.
Cloud contamination and sensor degradation affect NDVI time-series continuity, particularly in mountainous terrain.
The Rangeland Analysis Platform uses Landsat at 30m but has limited validation in Central Asian rangelands compared to North American rangelands.

claim: Mongolia's dzud events — extreme winters following dry summers that prevent livestock from accessing forage — killed over 10 million livestock in the 2009-2010 dzud alone, and climate change is increasing dzud frequency and severity.

source.title: Dzud — a natural disaster in Mongolia and its impact on pastoral livelihoods

method: Reviewed dzud event documentation, livestock mortality statistics, climate-change projections, and pastoral livelihood impact analysis for Mongolia and Central Asia.
limitations: Livestock mortality figures are from government reports and may undercount losses in remote herding communities.
Dzud classification varies — some studies use meteorological thresholds, others use livestock mortality outcomes.

The relationship between grassland degradation and dzud vulnerability is complex — degraded pastures reduce livestock body condition pre-dzud but dzud severity depends on winter weather, not just summer grazing.
Mongolia data is more available than Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, or Kyrgyzstan — Central Asian pastoralist data gaps are severe outside Mongolia.
